skill.name: nano-banana-edit
skill.description: Edit images with Google Nano Banana 2 (image-to-image edit endpoint) on RunComfy. Documents Nano Banana Edit's strengths (preserve subject identity, swap background, localize edits with spatial language, multi-image batch edits up to 20 inputs), the schema, and when to route to GPT Image 2 edit / Flux Kontext / Nano Banana 2 t2i instead. Calls `runcomfy run google/nano-banana-2/edit` through the local RunComfy CLI. Triggers on "nano banana edit", "edit with nano banana", "image edit nano banana", or any explicit ask to edit with this model.
skill.summary: Image-to-image editing skill using Google Nano Banana 2 on RunComfy CLI, with identity preservation, spatial localization, and batch support up to 20 images.
skill.author: runcomfy-com

security_audit.summary: The SKILL.md file is pure documentation describing how to use the RunComfy CLI for image editing. All 57 'external command' matches are markdown code blocks and inline code formatting — not actual code execution. The 11 URL matches are legitimate documentation links and the documented API endpoint (model-api.runcomfy.net). The 7 filesystem matches are standard CLI flags (--output-dir) and a documented token storage path with 0600 permissions. The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' finding is a false positive — lines 4 and 162 contain no cryptographic code. The 'system reconnaissance' match on line 38 is just routing/trigger logic documentation. The skill itself contains no executable code; it only documents CLI invocations for the user to run. An explicit Security & Privacy section confirms safe token handling, no shell expansion of user prompts, and a 2 GiB download cap.

low_findings.title: Documented CLI Command Execution
low_findings.description: The skill documents bash invocations of the RunComfy CLI (e.g., `runcomfy run google/nano-banana-2/edit`). These are example commands for the user to run, not code executed by the skill itself. The skill is documentation-only.

low_findings.title: External URLs in Documentation
low_findings.description: URLs to runcomfy.com, docs.runcomfy.com, and the model API endpoint (model-api.runcomfy.net) are documented for reference. These are the legitimate endpoints the user explicitly invokes via the CLI.

low_findings.title: Documented Filesystem Paths
low_findings.description: The skill references standard CLI output paths and `~/.config/runcomfy/token.json` for token storage. The token file is explicitly documented as mode 0600 (owner-only). No path traversal risk as paths are user-controlled CLI arguments.

content.user_title: Edit Images with Google Nano Banana 2 on RunComfy
content.value_statement: Image editing with AI models is complex and model-dependent. This skill guides Claude and Codex to route editing requests to the right model and generate correct RunComfy CLI commands for Nano Banana 2's image-to-image edit endpoint.
actual_capabilities: Document the Google Nano Banana 2 edit endpoint schema (prompt, image_urls, aspect_ratio, resolution, output_format, seed)
Guide single-image background swaps while preserving subject identity
Support batch editing of up to 20 input images with locked framing
Enable targeted spatial edits using positional language (e.g., 'left object only')
Route requests to the appropriate model (Nano Banana Edit vs GPT Image 2 vs Flux Kontext vs t2i)
Generate correct runcomfy CLI commands with proper JSON input formatting
limitations: 1–20 input images per call (first is primary, rest are auxiliary cues)
Long compound prompts cause drift — must be split into multiple passes
Multilingual in-image text edits are better handled by GPT Image 2 edit

use_cases.title: E-commerce SKU Gallery Generation
use_cases.description: Product photographers and e-commerce managers use this skill to batch-edit product images onto different backgrounds while keeping product identity, framing, and lighting consistent across 20 SKUs.

use_cases.title: Marketing Background Swaps
use_cases.description: Influencer marketers and brand teams use this skill to place spokesperson photos into themed backgrounds (cyberpunk, tropical, studio) while preserving facial identity and clothing.

use_cases.title: A/B Creative Variant Testing
use_cases.description: Ad designers use this skill to generate multiple output variants per image using seed locks and number_of_images, enabling systematic A/B testing of visual creative.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Background Swap
prompt_templates.prompt: Edit this portrait photo: Keep the subject identity, pose, and clothing unchanged. Convert the background into a rainy neon cyberpunk street.

prompt_templates.title: Targeted Object Removal
prompt_templates.prompt: Edit this still-life photo: Remove the leftmost object only. Keep the right two objects, the table, and the lighting unchanged.
prompt_templates.scenario: Spatial-localized edit removing a specific object

prompt_templates.title: Batch Product Gallery
prompt_templates.prompt: Edit these 20 product photos: Replace the watermark in the bottom-right with the text "AURA" in clean white sans-serif. Keep everything else exactly as in the input.
prompt_templates.scenario: Batch edit with locked framing for SKU consistency

prompt_templates.title: In-Image Text Replacement
prompt_templates.prompt: Edit this product photo: Keep the bottle, label, and lighting exactly as in the input. Replace only the brand text on the label from "ALPHA" to "AURA", same font weight, centered, white on black.
prompt_templates.scenario: Precise text element replacement on a product label

output_examples.input: User says: 'Edit this portrait to have a cyberpunk background'
output_examples.output: Routes to Nano Banana Edit endpoint and generates: a runcomfy run command with the prompt leading with identity preservation and ending with the cyberpunk background change.

output_examples.input: User says: 'I need to edit 15 product photos to remove the watermark and add my brand name'
output_examples.output: Routes to Nano Banana Edit with aspect_ratio and resolution locked, generates a batch command with 15 image_urls and a text-replacement prompt.

output_examples.input: User says: 'Generate a new image of a cat in space'
output_examples.output: Routes away from this skill to the Nano Banana 2 t2i sibling skill, since this is a text-to-image generation task, not an edit.

best_practices: Lead prompts with preservation goals ('Keep identity/pose/clothing unchanged') and end with the change — models honor what's stated first.
Use concrete spatial language for localized edits ('background only', 'the left object', 'upper-right corner') instead of vague modifiers like 'make it more X'.
Lock aspect_ratio and resolution across batch edits to ensure output consistency across multiple images.
anti_patterns: Long compound instructions ('change A and B and C and D') — increase drift per added scope; split into separate passes instead.
Edit instructions written in passive voice ('the background should be changed') — use imperative voice for better model compliance.
Missing preservation goals in the prompt — causes the model to subtly rewrite faces, branding, or composition.

faq.question: What is Nano Banana Edit?
faq.answer: Nano Banana Edit is the image-to-image edit endpoint of Google Nano Banana 2, a Gemini-family flash-tier image model hosted on RunComfy's Model API.

faq.question: How many images can I edit in one call?
faq.answer: Up to 20 input images per call, with 1 to 4 output variants per call. The first image is treated as primary.

faq.question: How do I authenticate with RunComfy?
faq.answer: Run 'runcomfy login' for an interactive browser device-code flow, or set the RUNCOMFY_TOKEN environment variable for CI and container environments.

faq.question: When should I use GPT Image 2 edit or Flux Kontext instead?
faq.answer: Use GPT Image 2 edit for multilingual in-image text edits. Use Flux Kontext for single-reference precise local edits like 'she is now holding X'.

faq.question: Is my prompt content safe from shell injection?
faq.answer: Yes. The CLI does not shell-expand user prompts — it transmits the JSON body directly to the Model API over HTTPS.

faq.question: Where are my API tokens stored?
faq.answer: Tokens are written to ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json with mode 0600 (owner-only). You can also set RUNCOMFY_TOKEN as an environment variable to bypass the file entirely.
